name: Kenya
tagline: From the cradle of humankind on Lake Turkana to the Swahili coast and a modern East African republic.
summary: Kenya holds one of the richest records of human origins on earth, its Turkana basin yielding hominin fossils spanning millions of years, including the near-complete Homo erectus skeleton known as Turkana Boy. Over later millennia Bantu and Nilotic peoples settled its highlands and lakes, while a string of Swahili city-states — Mombasa, Malindi, Lamu and Gede — grew rich on Indian Ocean trade and gave rise to the Swahili language and an Afro-Islamic coastal culture. The Portuguese seized the coast in the sixteenth century and built Fort Jesus, only to be expelled by Omani Arabs in 1698, after which the coast fell under the orbit of the sultanate of Zanzibar. From 1895 Britain ruled the interior as the East Africa Protectorate, drove the Uganda Railway inland, and settled the fertile highlands, dispossessions that fed the Mau Mau uprising of 1952–60. Kenya won independence on 12 December 1963 under Jomo Kenyatta and, after decades of one-party rule under Kenyatta and Daniel arap Moi, returned to multiparty politics in 1991, endured the 2007–08 post-election violence, and adopted a new devolved constitution in 2010.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: Kenya became an independent state on 12 December 1963, ending nearly seven decades of British rule that had begun with the East Africa Protectorate in 1895. The independence settlement was negotiated through three Lancaster House conferences in London between 1960 and 1963, where the rival parties KANU and KADU contested whether the new state would be unitary or federal. Jomo Kenyatta, jailed during the Mau Mau emergency and freed in 1961, became the country's first prime minister at independence; a year later, on 12 December 1964, Kenya became a republic with Kenyatta as its first president. The date 12 December — Jamhuri Day — remains Kenya's central national holiday.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 12 December 1963 from the United Kingdom; republic 12 December 1964

quickFacts.label: Human origins
quickFacts.value: Turkana Boy (Homo erectus, c. 1.5–1.6 million years old), found at Nariokotome in 1984

quickFacts.label: Swahili coast
quickFacts.value: Trading city-states such as Mombasa, Malindi, Lamu and Gede flourished c. 1000–1500 CE

quickFacts.label: Colonial rule
quickFacts.value: East Africa Protectorate from 1895; Kenya Colony from 1920

quickFacts.label: Current constitution
quickFacts.value: Adopted by referendum 4 August 2010; created 47 devolved counties

eras.title: Cradle of Humankind: the Turkana Basin
eras.period: c. 4 million years ago – c. 1000 BCE
eras.standfirst: The shores of Lake Turkana and the Koobi Fora badlands have yielded one of the world's most complete records of early human ancestors.
body: Few places on earth have done more to reveal the human past than the basin of Lake Turkana in Kenya's far north. Across the eastern badlands of Koobi Fora, geological deposits laid down from the Pliocene to the early Pleistocene have preserved a broadly continuous record of hominin and animal fossils. Decades of work, much of it led by Louis, Mary and Richard Leakey and their Kenyan collaborators, recovered the remains of several species of early human ancestors, including the robust australopith Paranthropus boisei and early members of the genus Homo such as Homo habilis and Homo erectus.

The basin's most celebrated find came in August 1984, when the fossil-hunter Kamoya Kimeu, a member of Richard Leakey's team, spotted a fragment of skull on the bank of the seasonal Nariokotome River. It belonged to a near-complete skeleton of a Homo erectus youth, catalogued KNM-WT 15000 and known as Turkana Boy, who had lived roughly 1.5 to 1.6 million years ago. It remains the most complete skeleton of its kind ever found, and transformed understanding of the body shape, stature and growth of our distant ancestors.

These discoveries underpin Kenya's claim as a cradle of humankind, and the surrounding fossil landscapes — Sibiloi, Koobi Fora and the Lake Turkana National Parks — are inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List. Long before recorded history, hunter-gatherer and later pastoralist communities lived across this region; from the third millennium BCE onward, Cushitic-speaking herders are thought to have entered northern Kenya, the first of many waves of peoples who would shape the country.

events.title: Turkana Boy lives near Lake Turkana
events.summary: A Homo erectus youth, later catalogued KNM-WT 15000, lives near what is now the Nariokotome River; his fossilised skeleton would become the most complete of its species ever recovered.

events.title: Hominins at Koobi Fora
events.summary: The Koobi Fora deposits east of Lake Turkana preserve fossils of Paranthropus boisei and early Homo, making the area one of the world's leading sites for the study of human evolution.

events.title: Cushitic pastoralists enter the north
events.summary: Southern Cushitic-speaking herders move into the arid lands of northern Kenya, among the earliest food-producing communities in the region.

events.title: Olorgesailie tool traditions and the Stone Age
events.summary: Sites such as Olorgesailie in the Rift Valley preserve vast accumulations of hand-axes and other tools, documenting hundreds of thousands of years of Stone Age life in Kenya.

figures.name: Kamoya Kimeu
figures.blurb: The most renowned of Kenya's fossil-hunters, who found Turkana Boy in 1984 and many other key hominin specimens, earning a central place in the study of human origins.

pullquote.text: Turkana Boy is the most complete early human skeleton ever found, offering an unrivalled window onto the body of Homo erectus.
pullquote.attribution: Summary of the significance of KNM-WT 15000

eras.title: Migrations and the Swahili Coast
eras.standfirst: Bantu and Nilotic peoples settled the interior while a chain of cosmopolitan Swahili city-states grew rich on Indian Ocean trade.
body: From around 1000 BCE the great Bantu expansion brought iron-working, farming communities into western Kenya, and by the early centuries CE Bantu speakers had reached the coast. They were followed and intermingled with Nilotic-speaking peoples — including the ancestors of the Kalenjin, Maasai, Luo and others — who migrated southward from the Nile valley region over many centuries, the Southern Luo arriving in western Kenya from around the fifteenth century. These layered migrations produced the ethnic mosaic of modern Kenya.

Along the Indian Ocean shore, a distinctive civilisation took shape. From roughly the eighth century onward, Bantu-speaking coastal communities trading with merchants from Arabia, Persia, India and beyond gave rise to the Swahili: a literate, urban, Muslim culture whose language, Kiswahili, is a Bantu tongue enriched with Arabic vocabulary. Between about 1000 and 1500 a chain of prosperous, largely independent city-states flourished — among them Mombasa, Malindi, Lamu, Pate and Gede — building in coral stone with mosques, multi-storey houses and pillar tombs.

These towns were nodes in a vast maritime network, exporting ivory, gold, timber, mangrove poles and enslaved people, and importing textiles, porcelain and glassware. The Moroccan traveller Ibn Battuta visited Mombasa and the coast in 1331 and described prosperous, devout Muslim communities; nearly a century later, in 1418, ships of the Chinese admiral Zheng He's fleet are recorded as having reached Malindi. The ruined town of Gede, abandoned by the seventeenth century, survives as one of the most evocative monuments of this Swahili world.

events.title: Bantu expansion reaches western Kenya
events.summary: Iron-using Bantu-speaking farmers spread into western Kenya, part of the wider Bantu expansion that reshaped much of eastern and southern Africa.

events.title: Rise of the Swahili coast
events.summary: Coastal Bantu communities trading across the Indian Ocean develop the Swahili language and an Afro-Islamic urban culture, founding settlements that grow into city-states.

events.title: Ibn Battuta visits Mombasa
events.summary: The traveller Ibn Battuta describes Mombasa and Kilwa as prosperous, pious Muslim trading towns, attesting to the wealth of the medieval Swahili coast.

events.title: Zheng He's fleet reaches Malindi
events.summary: Vessels of the Chinese admiral Zheng He's treasure fleet are recorded reaching Malindi, marking the eastern extent of the Indian Ocean trade that linked the Swahili coast to China.

events.title: Southern Luo migrate into western Kenya
events.summary: Nilotic-speaking Luo communities move into the Lake Victoria basin of western Kenya, the last major wave of the migrations that formed the country's peoples.

pullquote.text: The Swahili coast was a string of cosmopolitan, Muslim trading cities binding Africa to the wider Indian Ocean world.

eras.title: Portuguese and Omani Rule on the Coast
eras.standfirst: Vasco da Gama opened an era of Portuguese dominance symbolised by Fort Jesus, until Omani Arabs seized the coast and bound it to Zanzibar.
body: European intrusion began in April 1498, when Vasco da Gama, seeking a sea route to India, called at Mombasa and Malindi. Over the following decades the Portuguese fought to control the lucrative Swahili coast, and in 1593 they began building Fort Jesus at Mombasa, completed around 1596 to a design by the Italian architect Giovanni Battista Cairati. Built to resist cannon fire and command the harbour, it was among the first European-style bastioned forts erected outside Europe and became the linchpin of Portuguese power on the East African coast.

Portuguese rule was resented and never secure. In 1696 the forces of the sultanate of Oman laid siege to Fort Jesus; after a brutal blockade lasting some thirty-three months, the last defenders were overwhelmed in December 1698. With the fall of the fort, the coast of present-day Kenya, together with Zanzibar and Pemba, passed from Portuguese to Omani Arab control, and by the 1730s the Portuguese had been expelled from the coast north of Mozambique.

Through the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries the Omani Arabs consolidated their hold. Under Seyyid Said, who reigned from 1807 to 1856, the sultanate's capital was moved from Muscat to Zanzibar in 1840, making the island the commercial heart of an empire built on cloves, ivory and the slave trade. Mombasa and the Kenyan coast became dependencies of the sultanate of Zanzibar, a status that would shape the later partition of East Africa among European powers. Fort Jesus, by then much altered by its Omani occupants, still stands and was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

events.title: Vasco da Gama reaches the Kenyan coast
events.summary: The Portuguese navigator Vasco da Gama calls at Mombasa and Malindi en route to India, beginning a century of Portuguese involvement on the Swahili coast.

events.title: Portuguese build Fort Jesus
events.summary: The Portuguese construct Fort Jesus at Mombasa to a Renaissance design by Giovanni Battista Cairati, anchoring their control of the coast and the Indian Ocean trade routes.

events.title: Omani Arabs capture Fort Jesus
events.summary: After a siege of some thirty-three months, Omani forces take Fort Jesus, ending Portuguese dominance and bringing the Kenyan coast, Zanzibar and Pemba under Omani Arab control.

events.title: Zanzibar becomes the Omani capital
events.summary: Sultan Seyyid Said moves the Omani capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, making the island the centre of a commercial empire to which the Kenyan coast was bound.

events.title: Fort Jesus inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: Fort Jesus is added to the UNESCO World Heritage List as an outstanding example of sixteenth-century military architecture and a witness to the contest for the Indian Ocean trade.

figures.name: Seyyid Said bin Sultan
figures.blurb: The Omani ruler who shifted his capital to Zanzibar in 1840 and built a clove-and-ivory commercial empire along the East African coast, of which the Kenyan towns were part.

pullquote.text: With the fall of Fort Jesus in 1698 the whole coast of Kenya, with Zanzibar and Pemba, passed into Omani Arab hands.

eras.title: British East Africa and the Settler Colony
eras.standfirst: Britain pushed the Uganda Railway inland, opened the highlands to white settlers, and reduced Kenya's peoples to subjects of a colony.
body: Following the European partition of East Africa, Britain proclaimed the East Africa Protectorate on 1 July 1895, taking over administration from the Imperial British East Africa Company. To bind the interior to the coast and reach Lake Victoria, the colonial authorities built the Uganda Railway from Mombasa from 1896, reaching the swampy plain that became Nairobi in 1899 and the lake at Kisumu in 1901. Mocked in Britain as the 'Lunatic Line', it was built largely by tens of thousands of indentured labourers brought from British India, thousands of whom died, and it opened the Kenyan highlands to outside settlement.

The temperate, fertile highlands were declared 'Crown land' and reserved for European farmers, drawing British and other settlers who turned Kenya into what was called a 'white man's country'. Africans, above all the Kikuyu whose lands lay near Nairobi, were pushed into native reserves, subjected to taxes and pass laws, and recruited as cheap labour on settler estates. In 1920 the enlarged protectorate became Kenya Colony, a Crown colony, while a coastal strip leased from the sultan of Zanzibar remained a protectorate.

African grievances over land, labour and the lack of political voice produced early protest. In 1921 Harry Thuku founded the Young Kikuyu Association, and in 1924 the Kikuyu Central Association took up the land question. Jomo Kenyatta, who became its general secretary and spent years in Britain pressing Kikuyu claims, emerged as a national figure, returning home in 1946 and becoming president of the Kenya African Union in 1947. The settler colony's deep inequalities were building toward open revolt.

events.title: East Africa Protectorate established
events.summary: Britain proclaims the East Africa Protectorate over the territory that would become Kenya, beginning nearly seven decades of formal British rule.

events.title: The Uganda Railway is built
events.summary: The 'Lunatic Line' is driven from Mombasa to Lake Victoria, built largely by indentured Indian labourers; it reaches Nairobi in 1899 and opens the interior to settlement.

events.title: Kenya Colony created
events.summary: The protectorate's interior becomes the Crown colony of Kenya, while a coastal strip leased from the sultan of Zanzibar remains a protectorate.

events.title: Harry Thuku founds early protest
events.summary: Harry Thuku establishes the Young Kikuyu Association, one of the first organised African political movements against colonial land and labour policies.

events.title: Kenyatta leads the Kenya African Union
events.summary: Jomo Kenyatta, back from years in Britain, becomes president of the Kenya African Union, the leading vehicle of African nationalist demands.

pullquote.text: Mocked at home as a lunatic line, the Uganda Railway opened the Kenyan highlands to a settler colony built on African land.

eras.title: Mau Mau and the Road to Independence
eras.standfirst: The Mau Mau uprising and a brutal state of emergency hastened the end of empire, and Kenya won independence under Jomo Kenyatta.
body: Land hunger, dispossession and the denial of political rights erupted into armed revolt in 1952. The Mau Mau movement, drawn mainly from the Kikuyu but also from the Embu and Meru, took up arms against the colonial order, and in October 1952 the government declared a state of emergency that would last until January 1960. The conflict was brutal: the British detained well over 100,000 Kenyans, mostly Kikuyu, in detention camps and 'protected villages', and tens of thousands died. Though Jomo Kenyatta had publicly distanced himself from the violence, he was arrested, tried in 1953 in proceedings later widely condemned as a sham, and imprisoned.

The emergency, and the cost of suppressing it, convinced Britain that the settler order could not endure. Constitutional reform accelerated through three conferences at Lancaster House in London. The first, in 1960, conceded the principle of eventual African majority rule; the second, in 1962, and the third, in 1963, hammered out the independence constitution amid a contest between the Kenya African National Union (KANU), favouring a strong central state, and the Kenya African Democratic Union (KADU), which sought a federal 'majimbo' structure to protect smaller communities.

Released from detention in 1961, Kenyatta took the leadership of KANU and led its delegation in the final negotiations. KANU won the pre-independence elections, internal self-government — Madaraka — came on 1 June 1963, and on 12 December 1963 Kenya became fully independent with Kenyatta as prime minister. Exactly one year later, on 12 December 1964, the country became a republic with Kenyatta as its first president, and the rival KADU dissolved itself into KANU.

events.title: State of emergency declared
events.summary: As the Mau Mau revolt spreads, the colonial government declares a state of emergency that lasts until 1960, ushering in mass detentions and a brutal counter-insurgency.

events.title: Jomo Kenyatta convicted
events.summary: Kenyatta is tried and imprisoned for allegedly directing the Mau Mau, in proceedings later widely denounced as a sham; he is freed in 1961.

events.title: Lancaster House conferences
events.summary: Three constitutional conferences in London negotiate Kenya's path to independence amid rivalry between KANU and the federalist KADU.

events.title: Internal self-government (Madaraka)
events.summary: Kenya attains internal self-government under a KANU government led by Kenyatta, six months before full independence.

events.title: Kenya becomes independent
events.summary: Kenya gains independence from the United Kingdom with Jomo Kenyatta as prime minister; one year later, on 12 December 1964, it becomes a republic.

figures.name: Jomo Kenyatta
figures.role: Independence leader; first president of Kenya
figures.blurb: Nationalist leader of the Kikuyu Central Association and the Kenya African Union, imprisoned during the Mau Mau emergency, who became independent Kenya's first prime minister in 1963 and first president in 1964.

pullquote.text: The Mau Mau revolt, and the cost of crushing it, convinced Britain that white settler rule in Kenya could not last.
pullquote.attribution: Summary of the road to independence

eras.title: The Republic of Kenya
eras.standfirst: From one-party rule under Kenyatta and Moi, Kenya returned to multiparty democracy and adopted a new devolved constitution in 2010.
body: Jomo Kenyatta governed until his death in 1978, pursuing a pro-Western, capitalist path under the slogan Harambee ('pulling together') while concentrating power in KANU; by 1969 Kenya had effectively become a one-party state. He was succeeded by his vice-president, Daniel arap Moi, who made the one-party system formal in law and ruled for twenty-four years. Moi's tenure survived a failed coup attempt by air-force officers in 1982 but grew increasingly authoritarian, marked by political repression and the detention of dissidents.

Mounting domestic protest and foreign pressure forced change. In 1991 the constitution was amended to restore multiparty politics, and Moi won contested elections in 1992 and 1997 against a divided opposition. Barred by term limits, he stepped down in 2002, when Mwai Kibaki won a landslide that ended nearly forty years of KANU rule. Kibaki's disputed re-election in December 2007, however, triggered Kenya's gravest post-independence crisis: ethnic and political violence that killed more than a thousand people and displaced hundreds of thousands, ended only by a power-sharing accord brokered by former UN secretary-general Kofi Annan and signed on 28 February 2008.

Out of that crisis came lasting reform. A new constitution, approved by referendum on 4 August 2010 and promulgated later that month, curbed presidential power, entrenched a bill of rights, and devolved authority to 47 newly created county governments. Under it, Uhuru Kenyatta — son of the founding president — was elected in 2013 and 2017, and William Ruto in 2022. Today Kenya is the economic and diplomatic hub of East Africa, a regional centre of finance, technology and culture, even as questions of corruption, inequality and ethnic politics remain.

events.title: Moi succeeds Kenyatta
events.summary: On Kenyatta's death, Vice-President Daniel arap Moi becomes president, beginning a 24-year tenure that formalises the one-party state.

events.title: Failed coup attempt
events.summary: Air-force officers attempt to overthrow Moi; the coup is crushed and followed by tighter political control and a constitutionally entrenched one-party state.

events.title: Return to multiparty politics
events.summary: Under domestic and international pressure, Kenya amends its constitution to legalise opposition parties, paving the way for the multiparty elections of 1992.

events.title: Kibaki ends KANU rule
events.summary: Mwai Kibaki wins the presidency, ending nearly four decades of unbroken KANU government since independence.

events.title: Post-election violence
events.summary: Kibaki's disputed re-election sparks ethnic and political violence that kills more than 1,100 people; a power-sharing deal brokered by Kofi Annan is signed on 28 February 2008.

events.title: New constitution adopted
events.summary: Kenyans approve a new constitution that limits presidential power, expands rights, and devolves authority to 47 county governments.

figures.name: Daniel arap Moi
figures.blurb: Kenyatta's successor, who ruled for 24 years over a one-party state, survived a 1982 coup attempt, and eventually presided over the return to multiparty politics before stepping down in 2002.

pullquote.text: The trauma of the 2007–08 violence produced a new constitution in 2010, devolving power to 47 counties.

figures.name: Jomo Kenyatta
figures.role: Independence leader; first president of Kenya (1963/64–1978)
figures.blurb: The towering figure of Kenyan nationalism, jailed during the Mau Mau emergency and freed in 1961, who led KANU to power and became independent Kenya's first prime minister and president.

figures.name: Daniel arap Moi
figures.blurb: Kenya's longest-serving president, who entrenched and then eventually dismantled the one-party state, ruling for nearly a quarter-century before the peaceful handover of 2002.

figures.name: Kamoya Kimeu
figures.blurb: Celebrated fossil-hunter who discovered Turkana Boy in 1984 and many other crucial hominin fossils, making Kenya central to the study of human evolution.

sources.label: U.S. Relations With Kenya / History
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: Turning Pebbles: Post-Election Violence in Kenya
